role_hints.role: Payroll processing specialists / payroll and timekeeping clerks
role_hints.oews_basis: 43-3051 Payroll and Timekeeping Clerks — the signature occupation of this code — is NOT in the record's 4-digit 5412 top-10 (which is CPA-firm dominated: 13-2011 Accountants 34.2%); it sits in the unlisted tail. Within 541214 proper, processing/service staff are plausibly ~35-45% of wages (hypothesis)
role_hints.note: Data entry, exception handling, off-cycle runs and tax-notice research are core AI/RPA territory; already shrinking per client at the platform players.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-4051 Customer Service Representatives — in the 5412 tail, not top-10; hypothesis ~15-20%
role_hints.note: Tier-1 inquiries automate via AI chat; escalations involving money movement, tax agency notices and garnishments keep humans.
Distribution and the accountant-referral channel are the industry's real competitive weapon; relationship work.

role_hints.oews_basis: 13-1111 Management Analysts 4.24% at 4-digit plus tail; hypothesis ~10%
role_hints.note: Data migration and setup increasingly tool-assisted; complex multi-state onboarding persists.

role_hints.oews_basis: 15-1252 Software Developers — 5412 tail; concentrated at platform players; hypothesis ~10%
role_hints.note: AI-augmented rather than eliminated; minimal at small independent bureaus.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-3031 Financial Managers 10.32% + 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 5.1% at 4-digit; hypothesis ~10% here
role_hints.note: Tax-remittance compliance oversight and client relationships at local bureaus; owner comp inside SDE at independents.

source_hints.source: PayrollOrg (formerly American Payroll Association) surveys, e.g. 'Getting Paid In America'
source_hints.why: Trade-body data on payroll technology and process automation adoption.
Client counts, retention, pricing and AI product disclosures — the platform benchmark independents are measured against; also documents client-funds float income.

source_hints.source: NelsonHall and Everest Group payroll services market analyses (PEAK Matrix)
source_hints.why: Analyst data on payroll BPO pricing structures (PEPM) and vendor landscape.

source_hints.source: Software Equity Group and Houlihan Lokey HCM sector M&A reports
source_hints.why: Multiples for payroll/HCM assets; small-bureau tuck-ins occasionally disclosed.
The sparse independent-bureau comp set for buy_mult.

source_hints.source: Independent Payroll Providers Association (IPPA) membership data
source_hints.why: The independent-bureau segment's trade group — owner demographics and bureau counts if published.

capture_questions: Pricing is per-employee-per-month (PEPM) plus per-run fees — a software-like subscription where labor savings would normally stick as margin: does embedded fintech payroll (Gusto, Square, banks, vertical SaaS selling payroll at near-zero margin as a wedge) and ADP/Paychex scale pricing force PEPM deflation anyway, so the AI saving is competed away rather than kept?

Can independent local bureaus survive at all between the ADP/Paychex oligopoly (with client-funds float income independents cannot match) and embedded platforms that own the SMB relationship at the point of banking or POS — who owns the client, the bureau or the channel?

Does the accountant-referral channel — which often controls the SMB payroll decision — migrate to platform partner programs (Intuit, Gusto partner tiers), stranding local bureaus' main source of flow?

Could AI agents filing directly with IRS/state systems disintermediate processors entirely, or do tax-remittance liability (reporting-agent status), NACHA money-movement rules and float economics preserve the middleman role regardless of AI capability?
terminal_value_question: Payroll is already software — the human labor being sold is exception handling, compliance liability and trust in money movement. The terminal test: do AI agents plus government e-file/payment APIs let employers (or their accounting platforms) run compliant payroll with no third-party processor, or do remittance liability, multi-state complexity, garnishment handling and client-funds custody keep a paid intermediary — and if the intermediary survives, is it the acquirable local bureau or the platform oligopoly that keeps the economics? Distinguish AI cutting a bureau's processing cost from AI dissolving the bureau's reason to exist between client and government.
special_notes: Dataset oddity: labor_share is 0.8625 — extremely high, partly a vintage artifact (2024 wages over 2022 receipts) and partly because service-bureau revenue nets out pass-through client funds; treat margins derived from it cautiously and cross-check against ADP/Paychex segment margins (which are far fatter) and small-bureau benchmarks.

Role-mix warning: the provided 4-digit 5412 mix is CPA-firm dominated and omits this code's signature occupation (43-3051 Payroll Clerks) from the top-10 — build V from the hypothesis structure and flag it.

Scale oligopoly is the central structural fact: ADP and Paychex dominate and earn float income on client funds — a revenue line independent of labor content that small bureaus largely lack; the acquirable pool is independent bureaus facing simultaneous squeeze from platforms above and embedded fintech below.

Pricing-model note: PEPM subscription pricing is deflation-resistant per-unit but exposed to competitive undercutting by wedge-pricing entrants — model pi_dist on competitive pass-through, not billing mechanics.

Boundary confusion: PEOs and employer-of-record firms are 561330 (co-employment, gross-wage pass-through revenue distorts comparisons), staffing is 561320, and HCM software vendors report as software — keep bureau economics separate from all three; 4-digit surveys blend CPA firms in.

Regulatory load: IRS reporting-agent rules, state money-transmission licensing and NACHA rules are real barriers to entry but also fixed-cost burdens on small targets — cuts both ways for a roll-up thesis.
